Idiom (Gallurese): A bòna fami no s’agatta malu ricattu
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Proverbial negative clause with impersonal/reflexive construction; prepositional phrase fronting, negation no, verb agattà in present indicative.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When someone truly needs something, they are less picky and accept what is available.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ca ha bisognu s’adatta a lu chi trova.
Usage Contexts: daily speech; family advice; food; scarcity; humorous pragmatism
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rural Gallurese food wisdom: hunger, household economy, and pastoral modesty make usefulness more important than refinement.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Gallurese): [a ˈβɔna ˈfami no zaˈɣatta ˈmalu riˈkattu]
First Example Sentence: No ti lagnà di lu pani duru: a bòna fami no s’agatta malu ricattu.
Second Example Sentence: Dopu tuttu lu trabaddu in campagna, a bòna fami no s’agatta malu ricattu.
Third Example Sentence: Quandu semu ghjunti tardi a casa, minn’aghju arricurdata: a bòna fami no s’agatta malu ricattu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical proverbial sentence with paired noun phrases; plural abstract complement and adjectival agreement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not waste attention on foolish or offensive words; ignore nonsense.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: No ascultà li discorsi senza sensu.
Usage Contexts: arguments; gossip; social media; family counsel; workplace restraint
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A compact oral formula used in village conversation to preserve dignity and avoid escalating quarrels.
First Example Sentence: Lassa ch’eddu parli; a paràuli macchi, arìcci suldi.
Second Example Sentence: In piazza anu dittu di tuttu, ma eu aghju fattu a paràuli macchi, arìcci suldi.
Third Example Sentence: Mamma mi dicia sempri: a paràuli macchi, arìcci suldi, e la paci ferma in casa.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival idiom with definite object; temporal subordinate clause with present indicative.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Act at the right moment while conditions are favorable.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Fà la cosa subitu, mentri ci hè l’occasioni bona.
Usage Contexts: business; negotiation; farming; craftwork; personal opportunity
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to blacksmithing and manual trades, where timing and heat determine whether iron can be shaped.
First Example Sentence: Lu clienti è prontu a firmà: appunziemu lu farru cand’è caldu.
Second Example Sentence: Pa cumprà dda casa, babbu dissi ch’era l’ora d’appunzià lu farru cand’è caldu.
Third Example Sentence: No aspittà troppu, chì li cosi boni si pigliani appunziendi lu farru cand’è caldu.

Idiom (Gallurese): Bisogna stirrì lu pedi cant’è mannu lu linzolu
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Impersonal modal construction with infinitive; comparative clause expressing proportion between action and limit.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Live within your means; do not spend or promise more than you can afford.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Bisogna adattassi a li mezzi chi si hani.
Usage Contexts: money; household planning; business budgeting; parental advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Domestic metaphor from modest rural households, where bedding and resources were measured carefully.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Gallurese): [biˈzoɲɲa stirˈri lu ˈpɛdi kanˈtɛ ˈmannu lu linˈdzolu]
First Example Sentence: No ti cumprà una machina chi no poi mantinì: bisogna stirrì lu pedi cant’è mannu lu linzolu.
Second Example Sentence: Pa la festa femu lu chi pudemu, chì bisogna stirrì lu pedi cant’è mannu lu linzolu.
Third Example Sentence: L’impresa crisciarà pianu pianu, senza scurdassi di stirrì lu pedi cant’è mannu lu linzolu.

Idiom (Gallurese): Bucca di méli, cori di féli
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal elliptical antithesis with two coordinated metaphorical noun phrases; no finite verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone speaks sweetly but hides bitterness, malice, or bad intentions.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Pari gentili, ma no è sinceru.
Usage Contexts: gossip; politics; workplace rivalry; literature; sarcasm
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses the traditional contrast between honey and bile, common in Mediterranean moral imagery, to expose false sweetness.
First Example Sentence: No ti fidà troppu di chiddu: bucca di méli, cori di féli.
Second Example Sentence: In riunioni sorridia a tutti, ma la ghjenti sapi ch’edda hè bucca di méli, cori di féli.
Third Example Sentence: Lu pueta l’ha discritta bè: bucca di méli, cori di féli, e manu sempri pronta a piglià.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative proverbial clause with present indicative; negation no; transitive verb plus direct object.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Those who are lazy or inattentive miss opportunities.
Usage Contexts: work; school; fishing; motivation; morning routines
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in coastal and rural labor rhythms: fishing, farming, and market work reward early action.
First Example Sentence: Alzeti prestu, figliolu, ca dròmmi no pidda pesciu.
Second Example Sentence: Lu postu bonu a lu mircatu l’ani presu l’altri: ca dròmmi no pidda pesciu.
Third Example Sentence: Pa imparà lu gadduresu ci voli custanza, chì ca dròmmi no pidda pesciu.

Idiom (Gallurese): Da lu saccu no vi pò iscì chi lu chi v’è
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative existential/proverbial clause with locative source phrase; modal verb pò and infinitive iscì.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: You cannot get from a person or situation what is not actually there.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: No si pò ottena ciò chi manca.
Usage Contexts: education; management; family expectations; realistic planning
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on storage sacks used for grain, flour, and provisions in agrarian Gallurese life.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Gallurese): [da lu ˈzakku no βi pɔ iʃˈʃi ki lu ki βɛ]
First Example Sentence: No li dumandà una risposta ch’eddu no sapi: da lu saccu no vi pò iscì chi lu chi v’è.
Second Example Sentence: La scola faci assai, ma da lu saccu no vi pò iscì chi lu chi v’è.
Third Example Sentence: In dda squadra mancani risorsi; da lu saccu no vi pò iscì chi lu chi v’è, puru cu la bona vulintai.

Idiom (Gallurese): Dugna nodu veni a lu pèttini
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Proverbial declarative clause; universal quantifier dugna, subject noun, motion verb, directional prepositional phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Hidden problems or unresolved matters eventually come to light.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Prima o poi li prublemi si scoprini.
Usage Contexts: truth-telling; accounting; family secrets; politics; investigations
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The comb image comes from everyday grooming and wool or fiber work: knots cannot remain hidden forever.
First Example Sentence: No falsificà li conti, chì dugna nodu veni a lu pèttini.
Second Example Sentence: Ani piattu la storia pa anni, ma dugna nodu veni a lu pèttini.
Third Example Sentence: Lu ziu suspirò e dissi: la viritai camina pianu, ma dugna nodu veni a lu pèttini.

Idiom (Gallurese): Faci lu maccu pa no pacà l’ustéra
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Present indicative idiom with predicative noun phrase; purposive clause introduced by pa plus infinitive.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone pretends not to understand in order to avoid responsibility, payment, or blame.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Fingi d’ùn capì pa scappà da lu duveri.
Usage Contexts: humor; tavern stories; negotiations; family disputes; satire
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Tavern-centered social humor: the ostaria/ustéra was a place of bills, excuses, jokes, and public reputation.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Gallurese): [ˈfatʃi lu ˈmakku pa no paˈka lusˈtera]
First Example Sentence: Quandu ghjunghji lu contu, iddu faci lu maccu pa no pacà l’ustéra.
Second Example Sentence: No hè sminticatu nudda; faci lu maccu pa no pacà l’ustéra.
Third Example Sentence: In uffìciu dici di no sapè, ma tutti vidini ch’eddu faci lu maccu pa no pacà l’ustéra.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Temporal/prepositional proverbial frame with universal quantifier; impersonal reflexive/passive-like construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: During Carnival, jokes and playful reversals are tolerated more than usual.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A Carrasciali la ghjenti accetta più faciuli li scherzi.
Usage Contexts: Carnival; humor; village festivals; teasing; social inversion
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Carnival proverb from Gallura: seasonal license allows joking before Lent restores seriousness and social order.
First Example Sentence: No ti ni piglià troppu, oghji hè Carrasciali: a carrasciali, dugna burrula si ’ali.
Second Example Sentence: Li maschari ridiani in carrèra, e tutti diciani: a carrasciali, dugna burrula si ’ali.
Third Example Sentence: Ancu lu più seriu di lu paesi s’hè missu a cantà, parchì a carrasciali, dugna burrula si ’ali.
